macro_segments.title: The Duration and Method of Announcing a Lost Object (Mishnah and Initial Gemara Discussion)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: How long must one announce a found object?
Rabbi Meir's view: announcement until neighbors know
Rabbi Yehuda's view: three Regalim plus seven days
Gemara: Whose neighbors must know about the lost object?
The neighbors of the place where it was found (Gemara resolution)

macro_segments.title: The Travel Time Problem: First Beit HaMikdash vs. Second Beit HaMikdash

micro_segments.title: The three-day and seven-day waiting periods and geographical reach
Rav Yosef's answer: First Beit HaMikdash had larger Jewish population
Abaye's objection: Second Beit HaMikdash population was spread out
Rava's resolution: Limited time demand on finder

macro_segments.title: What Information Must Be Announced When a Lost Object is Found

micro_segments.title: Ravina's inference: Must announce the specific item found, not generic 'lost object'
Rava's counter: May announce generically to filter out false claimants

macro_segments.title: The Three Regalim Announcement Practice (Baraita and Post-Destruction Adjustments)

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Original practice—three Regalim with seven-day waiting period
After Temple destruction: announcements in synagogues and study halls
When forceful people claim lost objects for the king: announce privately to neighbors
Story of Rav Ashi finding coins and the Roman who refused to claim them
The Stone of Claims in Jerusalem: central location for lost and found

macro_segments.title: Verifying Ownership: Identifying Marks and Suspicion of Deceivers (Mishnah and Gemara)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Must give identifying marks; don't return to known deceivers
Debate: Announce 'lost object' vs. announce specific item (Rav Yehuda vs. Rav Nachman)
Baraita: Progression from requiring marks to requiring character witnesses
Story of Rav Papa's father finding a donkey and bringing witnesses

macro_segments.title: Use and Maintenance of Found Animals: Work Animals vs. Non-Work Animals (Mishnah and Gemara)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: May work animals and use proceeds to pay for maintenance
Must sell non-work animals and hold the proceeds
Rav Nachman's time limits: 12 months for work animals
Three months, 30 days, or three days depending on the animal type and expense

macro_segments.title: Rights to Use Proceeds from Sale: Rabbi Tarfon vs. Rabbi Akiva

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Rabbi Tarfon permits use; Rabbi Akiva forbids use of sale proceeds
Gemara: The debate is whether one becomes a paid guardian (shomer sachar) or borrower (shoel)
Rav Yosef's position: All guardians of lost objects are paid guardians
Resolution: The debate concerns accidents—Rabbi Tarfon says you become a borrower
Halacha ruled like Rabbi Tarfon: one may use the proceeds
Abaye's limitation: right to use only applies to proceeds of sold items, not all entrusted money

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yehuda (duration of announcement)
Neighbors (whose must know about lost object)
Forceful people claiming lost objects for the king
Rabbi Tarfon vs. Rabbi Akiva (use of proceeds)
Shomer (guardian) types: shomer chinam, shomer sachar, shoel
Rav Yosef's position on guardians of lost objects
Rabba vs. Rav Yosef (status of guardian of lost object)
Stone of Claims (Even To'en) in Jerusalem
